description: Emotions, feelings, and states of mind

examples: Olen iloinen tänään. (I am happy today.)
Hän on surullinen. (He/She is sad.)
Älä ole vihainen! (Don't be angry!)
Olen väsynyt pitkän päivän jälkeen. (I am tired after a long day.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'ihastua' (to become infatuated). Used with 'johonkin' (into someone): ihastunut häneen.
examples: Hän on ihastunut sinuun. (He/She has a crush on you.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'rakastua' (to fall in love). Used with 'johonkin': rakastunut häneen (in love with him/her).
examples: He ovat rakastuneet toisiinsa. (They are in love with each other.)
Rakkaus on kaunista. (Love is beautiful.)

items.notes: Describes a person who is generally fearful or timid by nature. For being afraid of something specific, use 'pelätä' (to fear).
examples: Koira on pelokas ukonilmalla. (The dog is fearful during a thunderstorm.)

items.notes: The noun form. The verb 'pelätä' means 'to fear'. Partitive form 'pelkoa' is common in expressions.
examples: Pelko on luonnollinen tunne. (Fear is a natural emotion.)
Olemme onnellisia yhdessä. (We are happy together.)

items.notes: Derived from 'yksin' (alone). 'Yksinäinen' implies an emotional state of loneliness, not just being alone.
examples: Hän tuntee itsensä yksinäiseksi. (He/She feels lonely.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'innostua' (to get excited). Used with 'jostakin' (about something): innostunut matkasta.
examples: Olen innostunut uudesta projektista. (I am excited about the new project.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'tylsistyä' (to become bored). 'Tylsä' means 'boring' (the cause), while 'tylsistynyt' describes the person who is bored.
examples: Lapset ovat tylsistyneitä. (The children are bored.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'hermostua' (to get nervous). Can also mean 'irritated' depending on context.
examples: Olen hermostunut kokeesta. (I am nervous about the exam.)

items.notes: Note the 'ä' ending. Used with 'jostakin' (of something): ylpeä sinusta (proud of you).
examples: Olen ylpeä sinusta. (I am proud of you.)

items.notes: Note the 'ä' vowels. The verb 'hävettää' means 'to feel ashamed': Minua hävettää (I feel ashamed). 'Häpeä' is the noun form.
examples: Hän tunsi häpeää. (He/She felt shame.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'huolestua' (to become worried). Used with 'jostakin': huolestunut terveydestä (worried about health).
examples: Äiti on huolestunut. (Mother is worried.)

items.notes: Derived from 'rauha' (peace). Also used to describe places and situations, not just people.
examples: Pysy rauhallisena. (Stay calm.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'stressaantua' (to become stressed). A loanword adaptation from English/Swedish. The noun form is 'stressi'.
examples: Olen stressaantunut töistä. (I am stressed from work.)

items.notes: Used with 'johonkin' (with something): tyytyväinen elämään (satisfied with life). Note the 'yy' double vowel and 'ä' ending.
examples: Olen tyytyväinen tulokseen. (I am satisfied with the result.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'pettyä' (to be disappointed). Used with 'johonkin': pettynyt päätökseen (disappointed with the decision).
examples: Hän on pettynyt tuloksiin. (He/She is disappointed with the results.)

items.notes: Past participle of 'yllättyä' (to be surprised). Note the double 'l' and 'tt'. The verb 'yllättää' means 'to surprise' someone.
examples: Olin yllättynyt lahjasta. (I was surprised by the gift.)

items.notes: Derived from 'kateus' (jealousy/envy). Covers both jealousy and envy in Finnish, unlike some languages that distinguish them.
examples: Älä ole kateellinen. (Don't be jealous.)

items.notes: Derived from 'kiitos' (thank you). Used with 'jostakin' (for something): kiitollinen avusta (grateful for help).
examples: Olen kiitollinen avustasi. (I am grateful for your help.)
Toivo ei koskaan kuole. (Hope never dies.)
Lapset tuovat iloa. (Children bring joy.)
Onni on lämmin koti. (Happiness is a warm home.)
